name: Red-Black Tree
def: A self-balancing binary search tree giving O(log n) ordered operations via colour invariants.
problem: Ordered map/set with guaranteed logarithmic worst case and predictable rebalancing cost.
mechanism: Each node is red or black; invariants bound the longest path to ≤2× the shortest. Rotations and recolouring restore invariants on insert/delete.
benefits: Fewer rotations on update than AVL
limits: Pointer-based: poor cache locality vs arrays/B-trees
Higher constants than hash tables for point lookups
failures: Subtle invariant bugs in hand-rolled deletes
Memory overhead of per-node pointers + colour
whenNot: Point lookups dominate and order is irrelevant (use a hash table)
myths: "Always faster than AVL" — AVL is more balanced and faster for read-heavy loads
